content:
**Must know**
• A **disease** is a condition that disturbs the normal functioning of the body.
• **Communicable (infectious) diseases** — caused by **pathogens** (disease-causing microorganisms); **can spread** from an infected person to a healthy person.
  – Examples: common cold (virus), tuberculosis (bacteria), malaria (protozoa), ringworm (fungi).
• **Non-communicable diseases** — **cannot spread** from person to person; caused by genetics, lifestyle, diet or environment.
  – Examples: diabetes, cancer, heart disease, hypertension, allergies, asthma.
• Communicable diseases need a **source of infection**, a **mode of transmission** and a **susceptible host**.
• Prevention of communicable diseases: vaccination, hygiene, safe water, vector control.
• Prevention of non-communicable diseases: balanced diet, exercise, avoiding tobacco/alcohol, stress management.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Communicable disease** — a disease that can be transmitted from one person to another.
• **Non-communicable disease** — a disease that stays with the individual and is not contagious.

**Exam traps:**
• Diabetes and cancer are **non-communicable** — they cannot spread by touch or air.
• All communicable diseases have a pathogen; non-communicable diseases are **not** caused by pathogens.
• 'Infectious' and 'communicable' mean the same — use either term correctly.
teacherTip: Communicable = Catching (germ + spread). Non-communicable = Not catching (no germ, no spread). Always give the causative organism for communicable examples.
examTip: Compare at least four features in a table and give two examples of each type — this is the standard 4-mark distinguish question.

subtopic: 2. Pathogens — Bacteria, Virus, Fungi and Protozoa
content:
**Must know**
• A **pathogen** is any microorganism that causes disease.
• Four main types of pathogens:
  – **Bacteria** — single-celled organisms; examples: *Mycobacterium* (TB), *Vibrio cholerae* (cholera), *Salmonella* (food poisoning).
  – **Viruses** — much smaller than bacteria; need a living host cell to reproduce; examples: influenza, common cold, polio, rabies, HIV.
  – **Fungi** — cause skin infections; example: ringworm (*Tinea*).
  – **Protozoa** — single-celled animals; example: *Plasmodium* (malaria), *Entamoeba* (amoebic dysentery).
• **Antibiotics** work against **bacteria** but are **ineffective against viruses** (antiviral drugs are needed).
• Pathogens enter the body through air, water, food, broken skin or vectors.
• The body's **immune system** fights pathogens; **vaccination** trains the immune system in advance.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Pathogen** — a disease-causing microorganism.
• **Antibiotic** — a chemical that kills or inhibits bacteria.

**Exam traps:**
• Malaria is caused by **Plasmodium (protozoa)**, NOT a virus or bacterium.
• Antibiotics do NOT cure viral diseases like common cold or flu — a very common MCQ trap.
• Ringworm is a **fungal** infection, not caused by a worm despite its name.
teacherTip: Bacteria = treatable with antibiotics. Virus = no antibiotic cure. Protozoa = think malaria. Fungi = think ringworm on skin.
examTip: Always name the **specific pathogen** alongside the disease (e.g. Plasmodium → malaria). Partial names lose marks.

content:
**Must know**
• Communicable diseases spread through five main modes:
  1. **Air (droplet infection)** — coughing, sneezing, talking; tiny droplets carry pathogens. Diseases: common cold, influenza, tuberculosis, measles, COVID-19.
  2. **Water** — drinking contaminated water. Diseases: cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A, amoebic dysentery.
  3. **Food** — eating food contaminated by pathogens or flies. Diseases: food poisoning, dysentery, cholera.
  4. **Contact** — direct skin contact or sharing personal items; body fluids. Diseases: ringworm, scabies, AIDS (HIV).
  5. **Vectors** — living carriers that transport pathogens without getting the disease themselves. Diseases: malaria, dengue (mosquito); cholera, typhoid (housefly).
• **Prevention:** cover mouth when coughing, boil/filter water, cook food well, wash hands, use mosquito nets, keep surroundings clean.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Contagious** — easily spread by direct or close contact.
• **Vector** — an organism that carries a pathogen from an infected person to a healthy person.

**Exam traps:**
• TB spreads through **air**, not water — match each disease to the correct mode.
• AIDS spreads through **blood and body fluids**, not by casual touch, hugging or sharing food.
• A vector does not cause the disease — it only **transports** the pathogen.
teacherTip: AWFCV — Air, Water, Food, Contact, Vectors. Learn one disease example for each mode and you can answer any transmission question.
examTip: When asked 'how does X spread?', name the mode AND describe the mechanism (e.g. 'through droplets released during coughing').

content:
**Must know**
• A **vector** is an animal/insect that carries pathogens from an infected person to a healthy person.
• **Mosquito** — most dangerous vector globally.
  – **Female *Anopheles* mosquito** → transmits **malaria** (*Plasmodium* protozoa). Only **females** bite (need blood for egg development); males feed on plant nectar.
  – ***Aedes* mosquito** → transmits **dengue** and **chikungunya** (viruses).
  – ***Culex* mosquito** → transmits **filariasis** (elephantiasis).
• **Housefly (*Musca domestica*)** — carries pathogens on its hairy legs and body from garbage/sewage to food.
  – Spreads: **cholera, typhoid, dysentery, diarrhoea, tuberculosis** (mechanical transmission).
  – Flies vomit and defecate on food, depositing bacteria.
• **Vector control:** mosquito nets, insect repellents, draining stagnant water, covering food, proper waste disposal, screens on windows.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Biological vector** — pathogen completes part of its life cycle inside the vector (e.g. Plasmodium in mosquito).
• **Mechanical vector** — carries pathogens physically without the pathogen multiplying inside (e.g. housefly).

**Exam traps:**
• Write **female *Anopheles*** mosquito for malaria — 'male mosquito' or just 'mosquito' may lose marks.
• Malaria is NOT caused by the mosquito — the mosquito **transmits Plasmodium**.
• Housefly spreads disease by **mechanical** carriage, not by biting.
teacherTip: Female Anopheles = malaria taxi. Housefly = dirty legs on your lunch. Stagnant water = mosquito nursery — drain it!
examTip: Name the vector AND the pathogen AND the disease in one line: 'Plasmodium is transmitted by the female Anopheles mosquito causing malaria.'

content:
**Must know**
• **Vaccination (immunisation)** — introducing a weakened or killed pathogen (or its harmless part) into the body to stimulate the immune system to produce **antibodies** without causing the full disease.
• The body develops **memory** against that pathogen — future infection is fought off quickly.
• **Types of immunity:**
  – **Active natural** — body produces antibodies after actual infection (e.g. after recovering from chickenpox).
  – **Active artificial** — antibodies produced after **vaccination** (e.g. BCG for TB, OPV for polio, DPT for diphtheria-pertussis-tetanus).
  – **Passive natural** — antibodies received from mother through placenta or breast milk.
  – **Passive artificial** — ready-made **antiserum/antitoxin** injected (e.g. anti-rabies serum, anti-tetanus serum, snake-bite antivenom).
• **BCG vaccine** — against tuberculosis. **OPV** — oral polio vaccine.
• Vaccination protects the individual and helps **community immunity** (herd immunity) when most people are vaccinated.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Vaccine** — a preparation containing weakened/killed pathogens used to produce immunity.
• **Antibody** — a protein produced by the body to destroy specific pathogens.
• **Antiserum** — blood serum containing ready-made antibodies.

**Exam traps:**
• Vaccination gives **active** immunity (body makes its own antibodies), not passive.
• Passive immunity is **temporary** (antibodies borrowed); active immunity is **long-lasting**.
• BCG is for **tuberculosis**, not polio — match each vaccine to its disease.
teacherTip: Active = body makes antibodies (vaccine). Passive = borrow someone else's antibodies (antiserum). Vaccine = training drill; antiserum = emergency backup.
examTip: For immunity type questions, state WHO produces the antibodies (own body vs injected) and give one example of each type.

content:
**Must know**
• **HIV** (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) attacks and weakens the body's **immune system**, especially **T-lymphocytes**.
• **AIDS** (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is the **advanced stage** of HIV infection — the body cannot fight even minor infections.
• HIV is **NOT** spread by: hugging, shaking hands, sharing food, mosquito bites, or using the same toilet.
• HIV **IS** spread through:
  – Unprotected sexual contact with an infected person.
  – Transfusion of infected **blood**.
  – Sharing **needles/syringes** (drug users).
  – From infected mother to baby during **pregnancy, childbirth or breastfeeding**.
• There is **no complete cure** for AIDS, but antiretroviral drugs can control HIV.
• **Prevention:** safe sex, screened blood transfusions, sterile needles, awareness and education; avoid discrimination against HIV-positive persons.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **HIV** — the virus that causes immune deficiency.
• **AIDS** — the syndrome (collection of diseases) resulting from severe immune suppression by HIV.

**Exam traps:**
• AIDS is **not** communicable by casual contact — this is tested in almost every paper.
• HIV is a **virus** — antibiotics do not work against it.
• AIDS is **acquired** (not inherited at birth unless mother transmits HIV) — the 'A' stands for Acquired.
teacherTip: HIV = virus attacks immunity. AIDS = end stage when body loses all defence. Mosquitoes do NOT carry HIV — blood contact only.
examTip: List modes of transmission AND modes that do NOT transmit — examiners award marks for both sides.

content:
**Must know**
• **Lifestyle diseases** are non-communicable diseases linked to unhealthy habits and modern living.
• Common lifestyle diseases and their causes:
  – **Diabetes** — excess sugar intake, obesity, lack of exercise.
  – **Heart disease / hypertension** — high-fat diet, smoking, stress, lack of exercise.
  – **Cancer** — tobacco, alcohol, radiation, certain chemicals, genetic factors.
  – **Obesity** — overeating, junk food, sedentary lifestyle.
  – **Lung diseases** — smoking (chronic bronchitis, emphysema, lung cancer).
• **Prevention:**
  – Balanced diet rich in fruits, vegetables and fibre; limit sugar, salt and saturated fats.
  – Regular physical exercise (at least 30 minutes daily).
  – Avoid tobacco, alcohol and substance abuse.
  – Manage stress; get adequate sleep.
  – Regular health check-ups after age 40.
• **Balanced diet** provides carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, water and roughage in correct proportions.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Lifestyle disease** — a non-communicable disease caused mainly by unhealthy daily habits.
• **Sedentary lifestyle** — involving little physical activity.

**Exam traps:**
• Lifestyle diseases are **non-communicable** — you cannot 'catch' diabetes from someone.
• Smoking causes **lung** diseases, not liver diseases — match organ to habit.
• Prevention answers must be **specific** (e.g. 'exercise') not vague (e.g. 'be healthy').
teacherTip: Junk food + no exercise + smoking = lifestyle disease recipe. Prevention is the mirror image: eat well, move more, never smoke.
examTip: Link each lifestyle disease to at least one cause AND one preventive measure — cause-prevention pairs score full marks.

content:
**Must know**
• **First aid** — immediate, temporary help given to an injured or ill person **before** professional medical help arrives. Goal: **stabilise**, not cure.
• **Burns:**
  – **Do:** Cool the burn under **running cold water** for at least **10–20 minutes**.
  – **Do:** Cover loosely with a clean, dry dressing.
  – **Do:** Seek medical help for severe/large burns.
  – **Don't:** Apply ice directly, butter, oil, toothpaste or break blisters.
  – **Don't:** Remove clothing stuck to the burn.
• **Bleeding:**
  – **Do:** Press a **clean cloth** firmly on the wound (direct pressure).
  – **Do:** **Raise** the injured limb above heart level to reduce blood flow.
  – **Do:** Keep the person calm and lying down.
  – **Don't:** Remove embedded objects (glass, knife) — stabilise and seek help.
  – **Don't:** Use a tourniquet unless trained (can cause tissue damage).
• For **nosebleed:** sit upright, lean forward, pinch the soft part of the nose for 10 minutes.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **First aid** — emergency care given at the site of injury.
• **Direct pressure** — applying firm force on a wound to stop bleeding.

**Exam traps:**
• Ice directly on burns causes **further tissue damage** — use cool running water instead.
• Removing a deeply embedded object can worsen bleeding — **stabilise in place**.
• For nosebleed, lean **forward** (not backward) to prevent blood entering the throat.
teacherTip: Burns = COOL water, not ice. Bleeding = PRESSURE + ELEVATE. Always state what NOT to do — ICSE loves 'give reasons why we should not…' questions.
examTip: Structure answers as DO / DON'T lists. Each correct point with a brief reason earns one mark.

subtopic: 9. First Aid — CPR and Emergency Response Basics
content:
**Must know**
• **CPR** (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) — an emergency procedure when someone's **heart has stopped** or they are **not breathing**.
• **When to use CPR:** person is unconscious, not breathing normally, and has no pulse.
• **Basic steps (Class 8 level):**
  1. Check for **responsiveness** — tap shoulder, call loudly.
  2. Call for **help** — shout for an adult, dial emergency number (112 in India).
  3. Place person on a **firm, flat surface** on their back.
  4. Give **chest compressions** — centre of chest, push hard and fast (about 100–120 per minute, depth ~5 cm for adults).
  5. If trained, alternate compressions with **rescue breaths** (30 compressions : 2 breaths).
  6. Continue until help arrives or the person starts breathing.
• **Choking:** give **back blows** and **abdominal thrusts** (Heimlich manoeuvre) for a conscious choking victim.
• **Fainting:** lay person flat, **raise legs**, loosen tight clothing, ensure fresh air; do not crowd around.
• **Fractures:** immobilise the injured part with a splint; do NOT try to set the bone; do not move the person unnecessarily.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **CPR** — emergency technique combining chest compressions and rescue breathing to maintain blood circulation.
• **Heimlich manoeuvre** — abdominal thrusts to dislodge an airway obstruction.

**Exam traps:**
• CPR is for **no heartbeat/no breathing** — not for minor fainting where the person is still breathing.
• Do not give water or food to an **unconscious** person — risk of choking.
• Moving a person with a suspected **spinal injury** can cause paralysis — keep them still.
teacherTip: CPR = Compressions keep blood flowing until the ambulance arrives. At Class 8, know WHEN to start CPR and the basic steps — you won't be asked to demonstrate.
examTip: For first aid long answers, always begin with 'call for medical help' and end with 'do not attempt to cure — only stabilise'.

content:
**Must know**
• **Public hygiene** — community-level practices that prevent the spread of disease and maintain a healthy environment.
• **Personal hygiene:** daily bathing, handwashing with soap (especially before eating and after using the toilet), brushing teeth, trimming nails, wearing clean clothes.
• **Community hygiene:**
  – Safe **drinking water** — boil, filter or chlorinate; avoid open defecation.
  – Proper **sewage and garbage disposal** — covered dustbins, regular collection, no littering.
  – **Vector control** — drain stagnant water, use mosquito nets, spray insecticides, cover food from flies.
  – **Food hygiene** — cook meat thoroughly, cover food, refrigerate perishables, wash fruits and vegetables.
  – **Isolation** of infectious patients; dispose of sputum and waste safely (TB, cholera).
• **Vaccination programmes** (pulse polio, BCG, measles) protect entire communities.
• **Quarantine** — separating infected/exposed persons to prevent disease spread.
• Clean surroundings reduce breeding grounds for mosquitoes and flies.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Public hygiene** — sanitary conditions maintained for the health of the community.
• **Epidemic** — rapid spread of a disease affecting many people in a community at the same time.

**Exam traps:**
• Handwashing prevents **both** contact and food-borne transmission — mention soap and timing.
• Open garbage and stagnant water are **breeding sites** for flies and mosquitoes — link hygiene to specific vectors.
• Public hygiene is about **community** action, not just individual habits — include both levels in answers.
teacherTip: Clean water + clean food + clean surroundings = three pillars of public hygiene. Every pillar prevents a different mode of transmission.
examTip: Suggest at least four specific public hygiene measures with the disease each prevents — examiners reward disease-linked suggestions over generic 'keep clean'.
